text: Three closed ports are visible on the right chest, with CO₂ insufflation being used to expand the thoracic cavity for the VAX thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection medial to the right phrenic nerve, incising the mediastinal pleura and continuing the dissection superiorly along the superior vena cava.
The pulsating mammary vessels are visible on the left side of the screen during the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon continues the dissection underneath the sternum, entering and widely opening the left pleural space. Thymic tissue is carefully dissected away from the ascending aorta and the innominate vein.
A 5mm trocar is inserted into the left chest to enhance visualization and facilitate dissection along the left phrenic nerve.
The surgeon continues dissection between the pericardium and the inferior portion of the thymus gland, carefully separating these structures.

The surgeon grasps and retracts the thymus towards the right side, facilitating further dissection around the aorta and pericardium. Thymic tissue on the left side has been excised, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon retracts the thymus gland inferiorly and to the right, beginning the dissection of the superior poles.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to sever ligamentous attachments, while the innominate vein is carefully skeletonized, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the superior pole of the thymus, extending the dissection into the neck area. The thymus is retracted to the right to facilitate access to the left superior pole.

The thymus is retracted to the left, allowing for dissection of the right superior pole. The surgeon continues to skeletonize the innominate vein, fully dissecting the tissue between the innominate vein and artery, with the innominate artery clearly visualized.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the superior pole, fully excising the thymus gland. The excised gland is placed into an endobag and removed from the chest cavity.
